system_prompt:
You are distilling one chapter of a guitar method book into a short summary.

The user will give you a chapter file with topic-organized verbatim quotes
plus the human-rationale notes for each. Write a one-paragraph summary (4-7
sentences) that captures:

  - what this chapter teaches (the techniques, concepts, rules, examples)
  - the master's characteristic moves visible in this chapter (if any)
  - the load-bearing terminology this chapter introduces

Reference the topic labels from the chapter file when useful. Do not invent
information not present in the chapter. If the chapter is biographical or
introductory rather than instructional, say so explicitly.

Output just the paragraph — no preamble, no JSON, no markdown formatting.
user_prompt:
Chapter 13: Moving Voices (Voice Leading) and Systematic Thinking

<<<CHAPTER FILE
# Chapter 13 — Moving Voices (Voice Leading) and Systematic Thinking

_Pages 69-74._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Treat chord notes separately

> One of the strongest ways to make chords connect smoothly is to treat the notes of each one
> SEPARATELY.

**Significance (p. 69).** Core voice-leading principle: chords as parallel lines.

## Voice and voice leading defined

> When thinking of the notes of the chords in this manner, each note is called a voice;
> the way that a voice moves from chord to chord will be called voice leading.

**Significance (p. 69).** Defines the chapter's key terminology.

## V7 to I centrality

> One important chord change that may use the principle of voice leading is V7 to I. Believe it or not,
> this one simple chord change is the basis for many, many sounds in music.

**Significance (p. 69).** Asserts V7-I as the fundamental voice-leading laboratory.

## Leading-tone resolution

> The 3rd of the V7 chord moves up to the root of the I chord in each example; this is common
> procedure for V7 to I.

**Significance (p. 69).** States the leading-tone resolution rule explicitly.

## 5th omission justified by voice leading

> leaving out the Sth in a I chord is acceptable in a case such as this.

**Significance (p. 69).** Permits 5th omission when voice leading is strong.

## Voice leading paramount for modern chords

> In moving modern chords, voice leading is of the utmost importance.

**Significance (p. 70).** Elevates voice leading to a top priority in modern harmony.

## Half-step approach chord rule

> Using a chord %
> step above or below the next chord is acceptable if good voice leading is employed.

**Significance (p. 71).** Permits chromatic approach chords when voice-leading-justified.

## Dominant follows m7 by 4th higher

> Quite often following a minor 7th type chord a dominant chord
> whose root is a 4th higher will be used.

**Significance (p. 71).** States the ii-V cliché explicitly.

## Anticipations acceptable

> Anticipations such as these
> are acceptable in most cases.

**Significance (p. 71).** Permits rhythmic anticipation of upcoming chord.

## Major 7 = major minus one root

> if you lower ONE root ina
> major chord that has TWO roots, you will get a major 7th chord.

**Significance (p. 71).** Specific systematic-thinking transformation rule.

## m(maj7) needs voice leading

> There are certain kinds of chords which almost necessitate the use of voice leading principles. One
> of these is the m# TYPE

**Significance (p. 71).** Identifies m(maj7) as a chord requiring voice leading.

## Systematic thinking defined

> This involves
> breaking down each chord into its tones, and analyzing each voice.

**Significance (p. 73).** Defines the chapter's titular methodology.

## Voice leading justifies all chord chart

> It is largely due to voice leading and systematic thinking that so many chords are listed in the
> reference charts.

**Significance (p. 73).** Justifies the reference charts: every chord earns its place via voice leading.

## All chords usable with voice leading

> With
> good voice leading, they all can be used

**Significance (p. 73).** Universal claim: any chord works if voice-led correctly.

CHAPTER FILE>>>
